summary:
The lingual nerve can get damaged when performing a mandibular nerve block because it lies close to the injection site. Other nerves may experience some degree of involvement too; however, they are less likely than the lingual nerve to suffer damage under these circumstances.
The main concern for injury during a mandibular nerve block procedure involves damaging the nearby lingual nerve rather than any specific secondary or tertiary structures mentioned previously. To ensure patient safety, careful technique should always prioritize avoiding direct contact between needle placement and sensitive anatomical areas such as where branches from major nerves converge near critical tissues within oral cavity walls adjacent to jawbone regions targeted by local anesthetic administration procedures involving inferior alveolar canal access pathways used here specifically targeting trigeminal ganglion innervation via branch connections extending into lower face region through various neural networks including those supplying sensation below chin area down towards tongue base location served primarily by lingual nerve pathway anatomy described above.
